§5.1 Spaces of Vectors

Definition 5.1.1.

A set which linear combination of a kind of vectors is closed. In other words, a set of vectors satisfying certain requirements.

Example 5.1.2.

is closed for vectors of exactly two real components, or consists all vectors consisting of two real components.

Definition 5.1.3.

In fact, there could be a vector space in that is not a that is still closed under linear combinations. Define such a vector space to be a proper subspace of .

Notice that every subspace must contain due to scalar multiplication by . And notice that every line passing through is also a subspace. Or, the more or less trivial subspace only containing is also a subspace.

Definition 5.1.4.

For a matrix , the vector space formed by the linear combination of its columns is called its column subspace, denoted as .

Example 5.1.5.

For a matrix , its column space is a plane passing through the origin containing vectors and .

Therefore, notice that is solvable iff is in .

The union of two subspaces wrt the same vector space may not be a subspace, as the sum property is not preserved. However, the union of two subspaces must be a subspace.

In fact, the vector space does not have to be made up of vectors, but any objects that add and (scalar) multiply (or analogs that retain key properties of them) in a closed set. For example, is the set containing . Also, the set of all real real matrices, is a vector space, and all real functions is a vector space.

Definition 5.1.6.

Generalized, addition must be commutative, associative, has an additive identity, and every element has an inverse; multiplication must be distributive wrt elements and scalar, associative (), and has multiplicative identity.

Here, I must show this photo.

Metric space is defined in Appendix C of my Multivariable Calculus notes.

Definition 5.1.7.

For a vector space , for any set of vectors , the subspace of spanned by is defined as the set of linear combinations of , denoted as .

Problem 5.1.1.

Suppose and are two subspaces of a vector space . Definition: The sum contains all sums , where and .
(a) Show that S + T satisfies the requirements (addition and scalar multiplication) for a vector space.
(b) If and are lines in , what is the difference between and ?
(c) Show that the span of is .
(d) If and , find the matrix such that , assume columns of and are all in .

Solution.

(a) Consider the sum of and , since addition is commutative, this is equal to . Since and , . since multiplication is distributive, and due to and being subspaces, and , so .
(b) is not necessarily a subspace. For example, being the -axis and being the -axis.
(c) Any linear combination of an element from and an element from could be mapped to an element in , in an injection manner. Take , since and , there is an element in corresponding to it. Additionally, any element in is a linear combination of and , specifically .
(d) Notice that is described by and described by , therefore . Hence, , also since that the columns of and have the same dimensions.

Remark.

In fact, is the smallest subspace containing both and .

Problem 5.1.2.

Show that the matrices and (with extra columns) have the same column space. But find a square matrix with smaller than .

Solution.

With the “column picture” of matrix multiplication, really is a linear combination of columns of , which is in .

The matrices I thought of had column space the same as its square; however, gpt hinted on the case where collapses to . For example,

whose square is the zero matrix.

Problem 5.1.3.

For what does an matrix has .

Solution.

and ‘s columns are linearly independent.
